title: Lists and stable keys

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Lists and stable keys; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Lists and stable keys
sources.claim: React list keys identify sibling items across renders so reconciliation preserves the intended component and DOM state.

canonicalExplanation.summary: React list keys identify sibling items across renders so reconciliation preserves the intended component and DOM state.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A todo list uses stable todo.id while sorting, insertion, and deletion preserve each row's input state. Your goal is to render and update a reorderable list while explaining key-based reconciliation. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Declarative UI and re-rendering; html semantics; JSX expressions and attributes; javascript collections methods. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: React list keys identify sibling items across renders so reconciliation preserves the intended component and DOM state. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Keys need only be unique among siblings, indexes fail for reordered editable lists, and keys are not passed as props. This draft therefore rejects: Array index is always the best key because it is unique during one render.

includes: Render collections with stable identity keys rather than positions that change with data.
Render and update a reorderable list while explaining key-based reconciliation.
excludes: Keys need only be unique among siblings, indexes fail for reordered editable lists, and keys are not passed as props.

outcomes: Render and update a reorderable list while explaining key-based reconciliation.

examples.title: Lists and stable keys: concrete case
examples.situation: A todo list uses stable todo.id while sorting, insertion, and deletion preserve each row's input state.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A todo list uses stable todo.id while sorting, insertion, and deletion preserve each row's input state.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Use stable data identity as key when items can reorder, insert, or delete; positional indexes can attach state to the wrong item.
examples.result: The result follows because Use stable data identity as key when items can reorder, insert, or delete; positional indexes can attach state to the wrong item.
